questions.question: What is sound?
questions.correct_answer: A form of energy which produces a sensation of hearing in our ears[cite: 4, 502].
questions.explanation: Sound is produced by vibrating objects and requires a medium for transmission[cite: 51, 549].

questions.question: How is sound produced?
questions.explanation: Vibration is a rapid to-and-fro motion of an object, which can be achieved by striking, plucking, rubbing, or shaking[cite: 41, 43, 539, 541].

questions.question: Why are sound waves called mechanical waves?
questions.correct_answer: Because they are characterized by the motion of particles in a medium[cite: 64, 562].
questions.explanation: Sound requires a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate and cannot travel through a vacuum[cite: 51, 52, 549, 550].

questions.question: What is a compression in a sound wave?
questions.correct_answer: A region of high pressure and high density[cite: 66, 67, 123, 564, 565, 621].
questions.explanation: It is created when a vibrating object moves forward, pushing and compressing the air in front of it[cite: 66, 564].

questions.question: What is a rarefaction in a sound wave?
questions.correct_answer: A region of low pressure and low density[cite: 69, 79, 124, 567, 577, 622].
questions.explanation: It is created when a vibrating object moves backwards, creating a region of low pressure[cite: 69, 567].

questions.question: Why is sound wave called a longitudinal wave?
questions.correct_answer: Because particles of the medium move parallel to the direction of propagation of the disturbance[cite: 105, 603].
questions.explanation: The particles oscillate back and forth about their position of rest rather than moving from one place to another[cite: 106, 604].

questions.question: What wave property determines pitch?
questions.explanation: Faster vibrations result in higher frequency and thus a higher pitch[cite: 185, 683].

questions.question: What wave property determines loudness?
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on the force with which an object is made to vibrate; greater force produces larger amplitude and louder sound[cite: 200, 215, 698, 713].

questions.question: What is an echo?
questions.correct_answer: The repetition of sound caused by the reflection of sound waves from an obstacle[cite: 299, 300, 797, 798].
questions.explanation: To hear a distinct echo, the time interval between the original and reflected sound must be at least 0.1 s[cite: 301, 799].

questions.question: What is the audible range for human beings?
questions.explanation: Frequencies below 20 Hz are infrasonic, and those above 20 kHz are ultrasonic[cite: 350, 355, 848, 853].

notes.title: Wave Equation
notes.content: The speed of sound ($v$), frequency ($v$), and wavelength ($\lambda$) are related by the equation: $v = \lambda v$[cite: 233, 449, 731, 947].

notes.title: Speed of Sound
notes.content: Speed of sound depends on the nature and temperature of the medium; it increases with temperature and is generally fastest in solids and slowest in gases[cite: 264, 265, 266, 450, 762, 763, 764, 948].
notes.source_section: 11.2.3 Speed of Sound in Different Media

notes.title: Reverberation
notes.content: The persistence of sound in a big hall due to repeated reflections is called reverberation[cite: 309, 454, 807, 952]. It is reduced using sound-absorbent materials like compressed fibreboard or heavy drapes[cite: 311, 809].

facts.fact: Sound cannot travel through a vacuum; it requires a material medium to propagate[cite: 51, 549].
The sensation of sound persists in our brain for about 0.1 s[cite: 300, 453, 798, 951].
Ultrasound is used in medical imaging (ultrasonography) and for cleaning hard-to-reach electronic components[cite: 374, 410, 434, 932].
